bottle-bill.title: Massachusetts Bottle Bill (M.G.L. c. 94, § 321–327)
bottle-bill.summary: Requires a 5-cent deposit on carbonated soft drink containers (glass, metal, plastic) sold in Massachusetts. Consumers redeem deposits at retailers or redemption centers.
key_requirements: Distributors must collect deposits from retailers
Retailers must accept returns and refund deposits
Containers must display Massachusetts deposit mark
Unclaimed deposits are remitted to the state after 30 days

sugar-tax.title: No Statewide Beverage Sugar Tax (as of 2024)
sugar-tax.summary: Massachusetts does not currently impose a statewide excise tax on sugar-sweetened beverages. Several municipalities have explored local ordinances, but none have passed as of 2024.
notes: Boston City Council explored a sugar tax proposal in 2022 — did not advance
State legislature has seen periodic bills filed; none enacted
Coca-Cola products are subject to standard Massachusetts 6.25% sales tax when sold as a prepared beverage

labeling.title: Beverage Labeling Requirements (105 CMR 500–599)
labeling.summary: Massachusetts adopts FDA federal labeling standards for packaged beverages. Additional state rules apply to caffeine disclosures in energy drinks sold at retail.
key_requirements: Nutrition facts panel required per FDA 21 CFR Part 101
Ingredient list in descending order by weight
Energy drinks with caffeine > 71mg per 12oz must display caffeine content on label

school-sales.title: School Nutrition Standards (603 CMR 29.00)
school-sales.summary: Restricts the sale of sugary beverages in Massachusetts public schools during the school day. Applies to vending machines, school stores, and a la carte lines.
restrictions: No sugar-sweetened sodas sold during school hours (K–12)
Allowed beverages: water, plain milk, 100% juice (≤8oz for elementary, ≤12oz for secondary)
Sports drinks permitted only for competitive athletes during/after practice
school-sales.agency: Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education (DESE)
